title: Granite Walkways and Bicycle Lanes: How to Safely Load a Moving Truck on the New 60-Meter Boulevards
excerpt: 60-meter boulevards: granite curb loading, bike lane rules, truck positioning, pedestrian safety, dollies, ramps, and legal staging on redesigned Addis corridors.
content:
Granite walkways and bicycle lanes: how to safely load a moving truck on the new 60-meter boulevards

Addis is gaining grand boulevards—roughly sixty meters of right-of-way with granite walkways, planted medians, and dedicated bicycle lanes. They look elegant on ribbon-cutting day. On moving day, they are a geometry puzzle: where may a truck legally stop, how do dollies cross bike lanes without endangering cyclists, and how do you protect polished stone from fridge dollies and strap tails?

This guide is for households and movers loading on redesigned corridors: safety, legality, and efficiency on wide streets that punish improvised parking.

Read the boulevard cross-section before you park

New layouts typically separate:

  • Vehicle lanes: Often multiple each direction with clear no-stopping zones.
  • Bicycle lanes: Physically or visually separated—never treat them as staging space.
  • Granite walkways: Durable but slippery when wet; edges chip under point loads.
  • Planting strips and medians: No idling, no ramp placement over root zones where prohibited.

Photograph the block and mark legal loading pockets—not the prettiest pocket, the permitted one.

Truck positioning rules that prevent fines

Wide roads tempt crews to angle across lanes. Officers on new boulevards often enforce strictly during showcase weeks. Plan:

  • Parallel park in designated loading zones only.
  • Use hazard lights, not improvised cones from the truck bed unless authorized.
  • Keep bicycle lanes completely clear—cyclists on new corridors expect continuity.
  • Rotate the truck if traffic police signal; courtesy prevents citations that clients inherit.

Loading across granite: protect stone and cargo

Granite pavers handle foot traffic; they hate dragging metal edges. Lay plywood sheets or mover blankets at the truck-to-sidewalk transition. Never drop heavy cases onto walkways—lower with control. Wet granite after rain is a slip hazard; assign a spotter when carrying tall items across stone.

Bicycle lane etiquette during carries

When the shortest path crosses a bike lane:

  • Post one crew member as crossing monitor during active load/unload windows.
  • Move in bursts when the lane is clear—do not leave dollies parked in the lane "for a minute."
  • Make eye contact with approaching cyclists; horns are last resort.

One blocked bike lane on a flagship boulevard becomes a photo on social media faster than an invoice dispute.

Ramp and lift-gate discipline

Lift gates swing into travel lanes if poorly positioned. Deploy only when the truck is fully legal and a spotter watches approaching traffic—including fast e-bikes in new lanes. Never extend ramps over granite edges at sharp angles; distribute weight with boards.

Pedestrian corridors and building entries

Wide sidewalks attract vendors and foot traffic. Keep a clear pedestrian lane where possible; propped compound doors need a monitor for phones and wallets. High-visibility vests help on showcase boulevards where officers expect professional presentation.

Shuttle planning when trucks cannot stop at the door

Sixty-meter roads may still forbid stopping at your exact gate. Stage at the nearest legal pocket and shuttle with smaller vehicles or hand carts. Measure carry distance during a pre-move walk—quote it upfront.

Weather and surface conditions

Morning dew on granite mimics ice. Dust season makes bike-lane markings harder to read—walk the route the day before. After rain, wipe dollie wheels before entering lobbies; mud tracks on new stone draw building complaints.

Contract and insurance notes

Damage to public walkway infrastructure can carry real liability. Confirm mover insurance covers third-party property, not only household goods. Clients should disclose fragile flooring inside; crews should disclose walkway protection they provide.

Choosing boulevard-experienced movers

Ask whether crews received briefings on new corridor rules—not only driving skills. Experience shows in where they park first, how they spot bike lanes, and whether granite gets boards before the first box.

Officer coordination at showcase corridors

Flagship boulevards attract patrols—crews should carry compound letters and respond promptly to reposition requests.

Point-load pads for heavy appliances

Refrigerators and washers need wide pads on granite joints—concentrated wheels crack paver edges over time.

Conclusion

Loading on new sixty-meter boulevards is a skill: legal truck placement, granite protection, bicycle lane respect, and spotter discipline. Wide streets are not wide permission to improvise. Plan the legal pocket, board the stone, clear the bike lane, and your move finishes without fines, scratches, or viral photos.
author: Active Movers Team
